python.explanation:
The transformation T^{-1} A S is a classic similarity/equivalence transformation in linear algebra. The function first checks invertibility by computing the determinant using Gaussian elimination — if either T or S has a zero determinant, no inverse exists and we return -1.

Matrix inversion is performed via Gauss-Jordan elimination on the augmented matrix [T | I]. We row-reduce until the left side becomes the identity, at which point the right side holds T^{-1}. The same logic applies to verifying invertibility during the process.

The final result is computed as two sequential matrix multiplications: first (T^{-1} · A) then (result · S). Matrix multiplication is implemented as a straightforward O(n³) triple-loop dot product. Rounding to 10 decimal places cleans up floating-point noise common in Gaussian elimination.
key_learnings: A matrix is invertible if and only if its determinant is non-zero — always check this before attempting inversion to avoid undefined behavior.
Gauss-Jordan elimination on [M | I] is the standard algorithm for computing matrix inverses, producing T^{-1} in O(n³) time.
T^{-1} A S is a similarity transformation when T = S — it preserves eigenvalues, making it central to diagonalization and change-of-basis problems.
Floating-point arithmetic introduces small errors in elimination; use an epsilon threshold (e.g. 1e-10) when comparing to zero, never exact equality.
For interview purposes, know that NumPy's np.linalg.det and np.linalg.inv are the production equivalents — pure Python implementations demonstrate understanding of the underlying algorithm.

numpy.explanation:
NumPy provides highly optimized LAPACK/BLAS routines for linear algebra operations, making this solution both concise and numerically stable. We use np.linalg.matrix_rank to check invertibility — a matrix is invertible iff its rank equals its dimension, which is more numerically robust than checking if det == 0 directly (determinants can underflow/overflow for large matrices).

The transformation T^{-1} A S is computed using np.linalg.inv for the inverse and Python's @ operator (matrix multiplication) for the products. The @ operator calls np.matmul internally, which uses optimized block matrix multiplication far faster than a naive triple loop.

A try/except around np.linalg.LinAlgError provides a safety net for edge cases that slip past the rank check, such as near-singular matrices that round to singular during LAPACK's internal factorization.
key_learnings: Use matrix_rank for invertibility checks instead of comparing det to zero — determinants suffer from numerical overflow/underflow on large or poorly scaled matrices.
The @ operator is Python's dedicated matrix multiplication operator (PEP 465), cleaner than np.dot or np.matmul for chained products.
np.linalg.inv uses LU decomposition internally — understanding this helps explain why it's O(n³) and why numerical stability matters.
For solving T^{-1} A S efficiently when only a linear system is needed, prefer np.linalg.solve(T, A @ S) over explicitly inverting T — it's faster and more stable.
Always convert results to .tolist() when the function signature expects Python lists — numpy arrays are not drop-in replacements in all contexts.
